speaker: interviewer
text: And this is tape number.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] Yeah. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother go into the hiding place that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the story I'm coming to now. Now, I may perhaps say at this point, there are three times-- types of camps, actually four. But I've never been in an extermination camp, which the fourth. But one is which were under the army, the Wehrmacht. At a spade-- a later stage and at one time, I was-- I've been in eight camps-- under civilian administration. And of course, the worst camp were under the SS. Now, in Klaj, we worked very hard. But the policy of killing people was left more to the SS-- anyway, as far as my experience goes. Now after eight days, the [NON-ENGLISH] gave us the permission to walk back to Bochnia, because we have heard that a few people were left. The train was full up. And a few other people were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with my father and the whole camp. And when I saw others walking barefoot-- it was August, I mean, beginning of September, very hot day-- and so I took my shoes off as well. But I shouldn't, because I'm a townie. And my soles didn't take kindly to that. When I arrived in Bochnia, I had blisters. I couldn't walk anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we arrive at home. And the house was empty. I couldn't walk anymore. So my father went upstairs. And he told me that there are certain signs the hiding place had been used. And he brought down two pictures, two photographs-- mother and sister in both of them. And one was written-- one was written in the back with a very shaky hand for me. And one for my father. If I had them today, this would be something more value than I can describe. Now, I couldn't enter the room anymore where we lived before. I stayed in the hallway. And I sat on some steps which-- the whole room was a little bit lower than the room. So it had one or two steps into the room. And my father went across to the blacksmith. Now, what he, what he told me was turning the knife in the wound. My little sister who spoke, speak Polish well and would have-- definitely, if she would have been older, she would have been able to disappear amongst the Poles and-- or Germans. Nobody would have been the wiser. She asked him in Polish, [POLISH]. My Polish is not without accent, either. Take me to the station. Take me to my father. I want to live. He was afraid. And I assume that the hiding place was to the front of the street. Maybe they were told by loudspeaker, somebody said, who won't-- who will not come voluntarily will be shot. Alternatively, she might have seen friends who we know in the street whom she very much regarded as intellectuals. Then my mother would be the type to say, oh, if they go, that's all right. We go. Anyway, they had left. And as I said, the-- the-- the knife was turned by this message that my sister had [INAUDIBLE] through the state. But she went back and the worst part is what I was, at the time, not thinking about but later learned about-- the trains at the time had arrived in Poland, a normal passenger train.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't know anything about the cattle trains. I didn't know anything about that they usually start being pushed to the capacity, standing up. But how can you-- how can you stand up? Who is sitting down first and who on top of you? That is why most of these trains didn't arrive without great, great losses. Did you know where your mother and sister had gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that point, no, no, under no circumstances. Later on, I learned about it. And I'm-- I'll tell you about it as, as it comes along, as the story unfolds. No, we clung to the hope that they were going to the east. The east was Ukraine. Ukraine has a lot of wheat and corn fields. And they needed-- harvest, people for the harvest. They needed people in the kitchen. They needed people for cleaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: There would have been plenty for them to do there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hoped. Now, I-- what they did, of course, they went straight to a extermination camp. And the name is-- actually, I told you this later. But I'll tell you now. The name is Belzec in Polish. I don't know how you pronounce it in English-- Belzec. But it's one of the lesser known camps. Because as far as I know-- also, figures vary-- only about, only about 250,000 had been killed there. There were later figures mentioned up to 600,000. But I will say at the time, I only learned about and I-- afterwards heard about it after the war, about 250,000. So Treblinka, Sobibor, Auschwitz-- they are the famous camps, and a few others. Anyway, my father, after so many hours, had to go back. And he bandaged my legs with the sheets in the house. And he would excuse me and said I would come as soon as, as I was able to. They shouldn't look at us as deserters. And who knows if he would have stayed, he could have been-- would have been caught later, what would have happened. You never know. It's just the first hurdle, so to say, which I hoped my sister and mother would be able to survive. Later, we wouldn't have known what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't go back with your father because of your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't because of the blisters. And when-- only a few years ago, I met the person who gave me the name of Fogelhut. He actually was in our group. And he never went back. But we didn't dare. He later went on Aryan papers. And he survived. But we had no contacts for such things. You have to be known-- know people anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So back in the camp, an ammunition camp, my father, myself, and another old man, we were in a way privileged. We did not work in the main camp, which was about a mile away. We were at the headquarters and also could sleep there. We-- my father, my father had some painting and drawings-- he was good at that-- to be done. And so did I, and another painter who was painting murals for the [NON-ENGLISH] the co-- the then commandant, to show off if he had visitors, generals, et cetera. But how did I get back? I have to tell you that first. Well, for one night, I stayed. In the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house. But after my father left, I went outside. And it was quiet, siesta, hot, everybody indoors, calm, as if nothing happened-- unreal. And a door opened not far away from us. A little boy came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he came from a family who was famous for having seven daughters. And the great joy they had when they had eventually a boy, and of course disappointment that he turned out to be a-- what is it? Not retarded-- Down syndrome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mongoloid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, Down syndrome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Down syndrome-- that's right. That's it. But they didn't love him less. And I called him, [? Mendele? ?] Yes? How are you? I'm fine. How is everybody? Everybody gone, except-- and he named two of his sisters. And they came out and invited me, wanted me to stay. I said I couldn't. And as I say, the next morning with bandaged feet-- I went on the side of my feet-- I went back. In the back of the house, there were all these branches. And I selected a stick. And people had a smile when I went into the train, walking on the side of my feet and, and with a stick. Anyway, I arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you managed to get a train back. But you had to walk there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a-- I got a train here. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't pay, I remember. I had no, no money. And there was no control. I would have found a way on what to say anyway. I arrived there. I reported. And what's going to come now sound-- will sound a bit cozy for [INAUDIBLE] at that time. One of the officers took me by car back into the camp where I could stay in bed, a thing which would be unheard of in later camps. For six weeks, I stayed. I was-- after three or four weeks, the blisters had, had gone. But I sort of malingered a bit longer. And some other fellow was ill. And at that time, I remember-- this particular part is not in my book-- I started writing in German, like a fantasy story. And I had written a few pages, , just an exciting story of somebody escaping from prison. And he was then found a double, and et cetera, et cetera. It looked like him. And it could develop into something interesting. I used some American names. It was going to be an American gangster story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I try to put the pages into a book and read it to the other fellow. And suddenly, I stopped. He said, go on. Go on. I said, it doesn't go on. He said, well, what you mean it doesn't go on? I said, I've writ it myself. By that at that time, I felt I could write.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next time I had the feeling to write but no pen and paper. That was about a year later. And by that time, I was in Plaszow. But to that, to that, we will come. Now back to the camp, I was-- my, my job was drawing graphs, gigantic graphs. If I they had something to do with the train movements, I don't know. They were put on several tables in the canteen. I was kneeling there. And with this funny instrument, I had to draw these graphs. And I was told by a German soldier-- his name was Bonats. And he treated me decently. And one time, he gave me-- he invited me to his place. And he gave me some-- something to eat. And it was after the war when I met him again. And I thanked him for, for the chicken. I found out it was, it was a hare, a rabbit. And-- but that is another story in Frankfurt. But I wanted to thank him. But he was very short. And as, as if with a bad conscience, he left-- he went away. But that's actually not in my book. That comes even later in a second book. Now, when the graphs were finished, there was not much to do. But I didn't want to go back loading and unloading ammunition. So the soldiers there were used to me. And whenever somebody saw me, I pretended to be busy. And that was enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: At one stage, I was given some work-- a number of essay books like they use at school. It was sort of to write the heading on an outside. Knowing the Germans for being, you know, being particular, was going to ask my father, should I write them at the bottom, in the middle, or at the top? So as I went out, the [NON-ENGLISH], the officer, came across. And I don't know what possessed me. I stood, I stood to attention. And he asked, yes, what do you want? Herr [NON-ENGLISH], and asked him, should I put this there, there, there? Oh, is that all you have to do? And he went.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evening, I had to go back to the main camp. And I had to work very, very hard. But in this ca-- particular camp, we didn't have much food. But we let's call it just about sufficient.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you slept in the barracks at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept in the barracks at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But he could come and visit me. At the time, we didn't escape yet. Because we had it good. We thought we're going to survive within this camp. Because we were needed. So we didn't much-- escape at that time would have been easy. But we didn't have to-- we didn't think about it. Now, the story unfolds. When the [NON-ENGLISH] sort of advanced in his rank, I thought with chagrin on my-- on, on my aching back, you know. Because I sort of, you know, worked very hard. But I was wondering why the others didn't complain so much. I didn't learn. I learned then that if you eat and you work, you get stronger. In the end, I got so strong that if the soldiers who looked after-- looked after us sometimes were not present, I was showing off. I took two of these bullets. But bullets is not right word. The other words for, for bullets. It's for, for cannons, the big ones. Anyway, they weighed about 100 weight each.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then two, and then at one time, even three. And I don't know if they were primed or not, but the way the boys threw them over their shoulder, I, I was frightened. And I did it slowly. But it was easier to sort of just throw them. Anyway, I, as I said, I don't know whether they were primed. The soldiers weren't worried, either. Because we had-- we worked on so many. And until a new one came and they shouted at us, because hey, you're going to explode us, you know, in, into the air. I said, well, we did it all the time. But he didn't allow us. We had to make it gently. We had to lay them down gently. Anyway, after a few weeks later, I was needed again by this soldier, Bonats, which I have already named. And he gave me another job. Now, there is an interesting interlude which I wouldn't have told if we had the time. But it's still interesting. One day, it was-- we were told anybody who has connection with America should come forth. And we did. They didn't tell us why. And suddenly, we ended in Krakow. I don't think there was a war at that time with-- between American and Germany yet. And we thought maybe we're going to be exchanged. Or we had letters from America still. And we hit our quota. And maybe we would be going. Now, what happened, we ended in Montelupich, which is a prison in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken, yeah. And we stayed there for a week. And details are in the book. It's not important about the rule about--

speaker: interviewer
text: But who was taken, your father, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and myself-- only two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two from that camp. And so it was a funny feeling, you know? First, we were these suppressed subhumans. And now, we were going to be citizens of the mightiest country on Earth. It was so exciting. But we didn't quite trust it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have family in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes-- well, she gave us-- she sent us the papers, and then hence, our quota, which I mentioned in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now for a week, we were in this prison. And then we were-- again, we had to gather down in the, in the yard. And SS-- a line of SS men stood in front of us and I thought were going to shoot us. Should we storm them, what? I'll make it a bit short. We had to go back. And on the way back, a girl who was taken from Bochnia, she told us they went to this-- they went to this big [INAUDIBLE]. I said, oh, hope-- I hope it's a nice camp. You know, I hope they don't work too hard. Of course, she couldn't tell me it was an extermination camp. She didn't know herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on your way back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the ammunition factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the ammunition factory. Now in the meantime, of course, there was always a [INAUDIBLE]. The picture of the painter was a marvelous sort of-- interesting. It was a tank, a Russian tank. And German soldiers jumped on the chang-- on the tank and threw the [INAUDIBLE]. They threw a grenade. And that was all, you know, as a mural. And whenever a general came or somebody, it was shown off.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, there was security like with the generals, only-- what should I say-- 10 times more so. Said, what do they need such security? Oh, somebody important is going to pass by next day. Near the-- we were not very far from the station. The camp was deep in the woods. But we were in the station, near the station. And suddenly, it was known that it was the fuhrer. Adolf Hitler himself was going to pass by to visit the Eastern Front. I said to my father, can we do anything? He said, no. For that, you have to have an organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was thinking frantically if we could do something. I went. And I had the run of the place. Of I went in, into the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] wasn't there at the time. I went into his office and looked around. And I even tried the jacket and the hat on-- his hat on, you know, like a boy does. And I had the idea, oh, if I would only get to uniform, maybe one could-- it could be useful at one stage or another. But I looked at the book to find out what trains were going. And at that time, I thought if you really were in touch with an organization, I could have helped them-- underground organization-- to tell them about this. Anyway, I could find nothing that could help me. But at night, I had no rest. And security was sort of evident everywhere. But this was a camp where I could walk backward and forward. It was-- it wouldn't have been-- what comes next wouldn't have been possible in a later camp. When I went to bed, I fell asleep. And I dreamt. And I was thinking so much about what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I dreamt about the time which I told you when I collected the wood. And I was so much afraid that it might fall between the buffers and derail the train. And I thought, why not try to play it for real? I stole out of the camp and-- at night. And I went along in an easterly direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: I picked up-- I knew where there, where there were some logs, a big log, one I would carry. And I went another two kilometers along the rail. And then I put the log down. And I saw there were-- I didn't have to do that. Because there were many more of similar and bigger sizes there. And I sat down. And I did what I should have done before-- think, think more clearly, clearly, what to do and what are consequences. My father, as I said, was against it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told him what you were going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I didn't. They were all asleep. And he-- as he was against it, I, I did it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had asked to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I didn't ask him anything. I just stole out of the camp. And then I was-- as I was sitting there, I heard noises and voices. Two soldiers-- I'm so sorry. The Germ-- the, the voices turned out to be German. And I withdrew further into the wood. And it was dark. I assumed there were soldiers on the other side. Came along-- I didn't know they had a dog with them or not. And it was obviously a patrol. And I could even hear what they said. But they didn't see anything important. They only spoke about a comrade who was lucky who broke a leg just before he was going to be sent to the Eastern Front.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as they went past, I went back to the rails. And I said, will I or will I not? But to go back and do nothing, it's just not my nature. And one patrol-- will there be more? I had to do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started heaping little stones on the rails and against the sleepers, and at first, a big branch across the rails onto the stones, and on the top of the branch with the big end towards the engine, bigger branches against the sleepers behind two of those, and then more woo-- more little-- tapering the wood, and you know, hoping sort of in my mind I'm-- I wasn't exactly experienced in that sort of work-- and hoping and imaging in my mind that that would lift the engine out of the, out of the rail and hopefully kill the [INAUDIBLE]. And well, having done it, it was already getting a little bit lighter. As I, I run back. And my father was awake. And he immediately scolded me. And he told me that you-- it's a very stupid thing I've done.
